lines.greek: καὶ σύ, φίλος, μάλα γάρ σʼ ὁρόω καλόν τε μέγαν τε,
ἄλκιμος ἔσσʼ, ἵνα τίς σε καὶ ὀψιγόνων ἐὺ εἴπῃ.
αὐτὰρ ἐγὼν ἐπὶ νῆα θοὴν κατελεύσομαι ἤδη
ἠδʼ ἑτάρους, οἵ πού με μάλʼ ἀσχαλόωσι μένοντες·
σοὶ δʼ αὐτῷ μελέτω, καὶ ἐμῶν ἐμπάζεο μύθων.
τὴν δʼ αὖ Τηλέμαχος πεπνυμένος ἀντίον ηὔδα·
ξεῖνʼ, ἦ τοι μὲν ταῦτα φίλα φρονέων ἀγορεύεις,
ὥς τε πατὴρ ᾧ παιδί, καὶ οὔ ποτε λήσομαι αὐτῶν.
ἀλλʼ ἄγε νῦν ἐπίμεινον, ἐπειγόμενός περ ὁδοῖο,
ὄφρα λοεσσάμενός τε τεταρπόμενός τε φίλον κῆρ,
δῶρον ἔχων ἐπὶ νῆα κίῃς, χαίρων ἐνὶ θυμῷ,
τιμῆεν, μάλα καλόν, ὅ τοι κειμήλιον ἔσται
ἐξ ἐμεῦ, οἷα φίλοι ξεῖνοι ξείνοισι διδοῦσι.
τὸν δʼ ἠμείβετʼ ἔπειτα θεά, γλαυκῶπις Ἀθήνη·
μή μʼ ἔτι νῦν κατέρυκε, λιλαιόμενόν περ ὁδοῖο.
δῶρον δʼ ὅττι κέ μοι δοῦναι φίλον ἦτορ ἀνώγῃ,
αὖτις ἀνερχομένῳ δόμεναι οἶκόνδε φέρεσθαι,
καὶ μάλα καλὸν ἑλών· σοὶ δʼ ἄξιον ἔσται ἀμοιβῆς.
ἡ μὲν ἄρʼ ὣς εἰποῦσʼ ἀπέβη γλαυκῶπις Ἀθήνη,
ὄρνις δʼ ὣς ἀνόπαια διέπτατο· τῷ δʼ ἐνὶ θυμῷ

interlinear: and-you, friend, for very-much I-see-you fair and tall,
valiant be, so-that someone of-men-yet-unborn may-speak-well of-you.
but I to my-swift ship will-go-down now,
and-to-my-comrades, who somewhere at-me are-greatly-chafing, waiting;
and-let-it-be your-own care, and of-my words take-heed.'
and-her in-turn Telemachus, prudent, answering addressed:
'Stranger, truly indeed these-things with-kind-intent you-speak,
as a-father to-his son; and never shall-I-forget them.
but come now, remain, though-hastening for-the-road,
so-that having-bathed and having-delighted your-dear heart,
a-gift having, to-your-ship you-may-go, rejoicing in-your-heart,
a-precious one, very fair, which for-you a-keepsake shall-be
from me, such-as dear guest-friends to-guest-friends give.'
and-him answered then the-goddess, bright-eyed Athena:
'do-not me still now detain, longing as-I-am for-the-road.
and-the-gift which to-me to-give your-dear heart bids,
when-again I-come-back give-it, homeward to-carry,
and a-very fair one choosing; and-for-you worthy shall-be the-exchange.'
she then thus having-spoken departed, bright-eyed Athena,
and-like a-bird upward she-flew-away; and-in-his heart
literal: And you, my friend, since I see you are very fair and tall,
be valiant, that men yet unborn may speak well of you.
But I will go down now to my swift ship
and to my comrades, who are surely chafing as they wait for me;
let this be your own care, and take heed of my words.'
Then prudent Telemachus answered her:
'Stranger, truly you speak these things with kind intent,
as a father to his son, and I shall never forget them.
But come now, stay, eager though you are for the road,
so that when you have bathed and delighted your heart,
you may go to your ship with a gift, rejoicing in your heart,
a precious gift, very fair, which shall be a keepsake for you
from me, such as dear guest-friends give to guest-friends.'
Then the goddess, bright-eyed Athena, answered him:
'Do not detain me now, for I long to be on my road.
And the gift your heart bids you give me,
give it when I come back again, to carry home,
and choose a very fair one; it shall be worth its exchange to you.'
So saying, bright-eyed Athena departed,
and flew away upward like a bird; and in his heart
translations.elizabethan:
And thou, my friend, since I behold thee tall
and fair, be valiant, that in after days
men yet unborn may speak thy name with praise.
But I go down now to my swift black ship
and to my crew, who fret at waiting there.
Look to thyself, and lay my words to heart."
Then prudent Telemachus made answer thus:
"Stranger, thou speak'st in kindness, as a father
would speak unto his own son. I'll not forget.
But stay a while, though eager for the road,
that thou mayst bathe and cheer thy heart, and go
down to thy ship with some good gift in hand,
a costly, lovely thing, to keep from me,
such as dear guest gives guest." The goddess then,
the bright of eye, Athena, answered him:
"Detain me not; I long to be away.
The gift thy heart would give me, give it then
when I return, to carry to my home,
and choose a fair one. Fair shall be thy meed."
So saying, bright-eyed Athena went from him,
and like a bird went darting up and away.
translations.modernist:
And you, my friend. I can see you are tall and good-looking. Be brave, so that men who are not born yet will speak well of you.

Now I am going down to my fast ship and my crew. They will be tired of waiting. Look after yourself, and do what I told you."

Then Telemachus answered her. He had sense.

"Stranger, you say all this kindly, the way a father talks to his son. I will not forget it. But wait a while, even if you are in a hurry. Have a bath. Enjoy yourself. Then go down to your ship with a present in your hands and a good feeling, something valuable and fine, a keepsake from me. That is what friends give friends."

Then bright-eyed Athena answered him.

"Do not hold me here. I want to be on the road. Give me the present when I come back, and I will take it home. Pick a good one. You will get your worth of it."

Bright-eyed Athena finished and left. She went up and away like a bird.
translations.storybook:
And you, my friend! I can see you are tall and handsome. Be brave, so that people who are not even born yet will say fine things about you.

Now I must go down to my swift ship, where my crew are waiting and getting very impatient. Look after yourself, and remember everything I have told you."

Then sensible young Telemachus answered her.

"Stranger, you say all this so kindly, just the way a father speaks to his son. I shall never forget it. But do wait a little, even if you are in a hurry! Have a warm bath and enjoy yourself, and then go down to your ship with a present in your hands, feeling happy: something precious and beautiful to keep, from me. That is what good friends give one another."

Then bright-eyed Athena answered him.

"Do not keep me here, for I am longing to be off. Give me the present when I come back again, and I shall carry it home. Choose a lovely one, and you will get something just as good in return."

And with that, bright-eyed Athena was gone. Up she went and away, like a bird.

murray.text: For thyself, give heed and have regard to my words.” Then wise Telemachus answered her: “Stranger, in truth thou speakest these things with kindly thought, as a father to his son, and never will I forget them. But come now, tarry, eager though thou art to be gone,

in order that when thou hast bathed and satisfied thy heart to the full, thou mayest go to thy ship glad in spirit, and bearing a gift costly and very beautiful, which shall be to thee an heirloom from me, even such a gift as dear friends give to friends.” Then the goddess, flashing-eyed Athena, answered him:

“Stay me now no longer, when I am eager to be gone, and whatsoever gift thy heart bids thee give me, give it when I come back, to bear to my home, choosing a right beautiful one; it shall bring thee its worth in return.” So spoke the goddess, flashing-eyed Athena, and departed,

flying upward as a bird; and in his heart she put strength and courage, and made him think of his father even more than aforetime. And in his mind he marked her and marvelled, for he deemed that she was a god; and straightway he went among the wooers, a godlike man.
